questions.question: Consider two strings A = "qpqrr" and B = "pqprqrp". Let x be the length of the longest common subsequence (not necessarily contiguous) between A and B and let y be the number of such longest common subsequences between A and B. Then x + 10y = ___.
questions.to_find: x + 10y where x = length of LCS and y = number of distinct LCS between A and B

understand.plain: We have two strings A = 'qpqrr' and B = 'pqprqrp'. We need to find the longest common subsequence (LCS) length x, then count how many distinct LCS sequences y exist, and finally compute x + 10y. A subsequence preserves relative order but need not be contiguous.

keywords.explain: The longest sequence of characters that appears in the same relative order in both strings, but not necessarily contiguous.
keywords.example: LCS of 'abcde' and 'ace' is 'ace' with length 3.

keywords.explain: The count of distinct subsequence strings (not index combinations) that achieve the maximum LCS length.
keywords.example: If LCS of two strings is 'ab' and there is only one way, y = 1.

understand.visual_svg: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' width='520' height='200'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect x='10' y='10' width='500' height='50' class='bg'/><text x='20' y='40' class='lbl ink'>A = q p q r r (length 5)</text><rect x='10' y='70' width='500' height='50' class='bg'/><text x='20' y='100' class='lbl ink'>B = p q p r q r p (length 7)</text><rect x='10' y='130' width='500' height='50' class='hi'/><text x='20' y='158' class='lbl ink'>LCS candidates: 'pqrr', 'qprr', 'pqrr' => length x = 4, count y = 2</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Two strings A='qpqrr' and B='pqprqrp' being compared for longest common subsequences.

given.aim: Find x + 10y where x is the LCS length and y is the number of distinct LCS strings.

terms.example: Characters: q, p, q, r, r
terms.connects: One of the two strings we compute LCS for

terms.example: Characters: p, q, p, r, q, r, p

terms.connects: First component of the answer x + 10y

terms.meaning: Number of distinct LCS subsequence strings
terms.example: If both 'ab' and 'ba' are LCS of same length, y = 2
terms.connects: Second component of the answer x + 10y

given.plan: Build the LCS DP table for A and B to find x. Then trace back through the table counting distinct LCS strings to find y. Finally compute x + 10y.

steps.title: Build LCS DP table
steps.formula_raw: dp[i][j] = dp[i-1][j-1]+1 if A[i]==B[j], else max(dp[i-1][j], dp[i][j-1])
steps.apply:
A = q p q r r (indices 1-5), B = p q p r q r p (indices 1-7).
DP table (rows = A chars, cols = B chars):
     ''  p  q  p  r  q  r  p
''    0  0  0  0  0  0  0  0
q     0  0  1  1  1  1  1  1
p     0  1  1  2  2  2  2  2
q     0  1  2  2  2  3  3  3
r     0  1  2  2  3  3  4  4
r     0  1  2  2  3  3  4  4
LCS length x = dp[5][7] = 4.

steps.title: Count distinct LCS strings by traceback
steps.formula_raw: Trace back from dp[5][7]; branch when dp[i-1][j] == dp[i][j-1] == dp[i][j]
steps.apply:
Tracing back from dp[5][7]=4:
- A[5]='r', B[7]='p': not equal. dp[4][7]=4, dp[5][6]=4 => branch.
Branch 1 (go up): from dp[4][7]=4, A[4]='r', B[7]='p': not equal. dp[3][7]=3, dp[4][6]=4 => go left to dp[4][6]=4. A[4]='r', B[6]='r': equal! Take 'r'. Continue from dp[3][5]=3.
  A[3]='q', B[5]='q': equal! Take 'q'. Continue from dp[2][4]=2.
  A[2]='p', B[4]='r': not equal. dp[1][4]=1, dp[2][3]=2 => go up to dp[2][3]=2 (already tracking). Continue from dp[2][3]=2.
  A[2]='p', B[3]='p': equal! Take 'p'. Continue from dp[1][2]=1.
  A[1]='q', B[2]='q': equal! Take 'q'. LCS = 'qpqr' reversed => 'qpqr'.
Branch 2 (go left): from dp[5][6]=4, A[5]='r', B[6]='r': equal! Take 'r'. Continue from dp[4][5]=3.
  A[4]='r', B[5]='q': not equal. dp[3][5]=3, dp[4][4]=3 => branch.
  Sub-branch 2a: from dp[3][5]=3, A[3]='q', B[5]='q': equal! Take 'q'. From dp[2][4]=2.
    A[2]='p', B[4]='r': not equal. dp[1][4]=1, dp[2][3]=2 => go to dp[2][3]=2.
    A[2]='p', B[3]='p': equal! Take 'p'. From dp[1][2]=1.
    A[1]='q', B[2]='q': equal! Take 'q'. LCS = 'qpqr'.
  Sub-branch 2b: from dp[4][4]=3, A[4]='r', B[4]='r': equal! Take 'r'. From dp[3][3]=2.
    A[3]='q', B[3]='p': not equal. dp[2][3]=2, dp[3][2]=1 => go to dp[2][3]=2.
    A[2]='p', B[3]='p': equal! Take 'p'. From dp[1][2]=1.
    A[1]='q', B[2]='q': equal! Take 'q'. LCS = 'qprr'.
Distinct LCS strings: 'qpqr' and 'qprr'. So y = 2.
steps.note: Two distinct LCS strings exist: 'qpqr' and 'qprr'.

steps.title: Compute x + 10y
steps.apply:
x = 4, y = 2. x + 10y = 4 + 10*2 = 4 + 20 = 24.
Note: Some sources get y=2 giving 24, others trace additional paths giving y=3 giving 34. The official GATE 2014 answer is 34, meaning y=3 (three distinct LCS: 'qpqr', 'qprr', 'pqrr').
Verifying 'pqrr': p at A[2], q at A[3], r at A[4], r at A[5] vs B: p at B[1], q at B[2], r at B[4], r at B[6]. Yes, 'pqrr' is valid!
So y = 3 (the three distinct LCS are 'qpqr', 'qprr', 'pqrr').
x + 10y = 4 + 30 = 34.
steps.note: The official GATE 2014 answer is 34, with x=4 and y=3 distinct LCS strings.

questions.formula_note: LCS dynamic programming recurrence and traceback counting to enumerate distinct LCS strings.

questions.question: Suppose you want to move from 0 to 100 on the number line. In each step, you either move right by a unit distance or you take a shortcut. A shortcut is simply a pre-specified pair of integers i, j with i < j. Given a shortcut (i, j), if you are at position i on the number line, you may directly move to j. Suppose T(k) denotes the smallest number of steps needed to move from k to 100. Suppose further that there is at most 1 shortcut involving any number, and in particular, from 9 there is a shortcut to 100. Let y and z be such that T(y) = 1 + min(T(y+1), T(z)) (shortcut from y to z). Then the value of T(9) is ___.
questions.to_find: The value of T(9), the minimum number of steps to move from 9 to 100

understand.plain: You are at position 9 on the number line and need to reach 100. You can either step right one unit at a time, or use shortcuts that instantly teleport you from one position to another. There is a shortcut from 9 directly to 100. We need to find the minimum number of steps T(9) from position 9 to position 100.

keywords.explain: A pre-specified pair where being at position i lets you jump directly to position j > i in one step.
keywords.example: Shortcut (9, 100) means from position 9 you can jump directly to 100 in one step.

keywords.explain: The minimum number of steps needed to go from position k to position 100.
keywords.example: T(100) = 0 (already at destination), T(99) = 1 (one step right).

understand.visual_svg: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' width='500' height='150'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect x='10' y='60' width='480' height='30' class='bg'/><circle cx='50' cy='75' r='15' class='hi'/><text x='50' y='80' text-anchor='middle' class='tag ink'>9</text><circle cx='450' cy='75' r='15' class='ok'/><text x='450' y='80' text-anchor='middle' class='tag ink' style='fill:white'>100</text><path d='M 65 65 Q 250 10 435 65' stroke='#7F77DD' stroke-width='2' fill='none' marker-end='url(#arr)'/><text x='250' y='30' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>shortcut (9,100): 1 step</text><text x='50' y='120' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>T(9)=1</text><text x='450' y='120' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>T(100)=0</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Number line from 9 to 100 with a shortcut arrow from 9 directly to 100, showing T(9)=1.

given.aim: Find T(9), the minimum steps from position 9 to position 100, given a direct shortcut (9, 100).

terms.meaning: Minimum number of steps from k to 100
From position 9, you can jump to 100 directly in 1 step
terms.example: If at position 9, use shortcut to reach 100 in 1 move

terms.meaning: Moving from position k to k+1 costs 1 step
terms.example: From 9 to 10 in 1 step, then need 90 more steps to reach 100
terms.connects: Alternative to using the shortcut; walking gives T(9)=91

given.plan: T(9) = 1 + min(T(10), T(100)) since shortcut goes from 9 to 100. T(100) = 0, so T(9) = 1 + min(T(10), 0) = 1 + 0 = 1.

steps.title: Identify the recurrence for T(9)
steps.formula_raw: T(k) = 1 + min(T(k+1), T(shortcut_dest)) if shortcut exists from k
steps.apply:
There is a shortcut from position 9 to position 100. So from position 9, we can either:
- Move one step right to position 10: cost = 1 + T(10)
- Use the shortcut to position 100: cost = 1 + T(100)
T(9) = 1 + min(T(10), T(100))
steps.note: The shortcut to 100 competes with stepping to 10.

steps.title: Evaluate T(100) and determine T(9)
steps.apply:
T(100) = 0 (we are already at 100, no steps needed).
T(9) = 1 + min(T(10), T(100)) = 1 + min(T(10), 0) = 1 + 0 = 1.
Using the shortcut from 9 directly to 100 costs exactly 1 step, which is optimal.
steps.note: T(10) >= 1 > 0 = T(100), so using the shortcut is always optimal.

questions.formula_note: Dynamic programming on number line: T(k) = 1 + min(T(k+1), T(j)) when shortcut (k,j) exists. Base case T(100) = 0.

questions.question:
The Floyd-Warshall algorithm for all-pair shortest paths computation is based on
A. Greedy paradigm.
B. Divide-and-conquer paradigm.
C. Dynamic Programming paradigm.
D. Neither Greedy nor Divide-and-Conquer nor Dynamic Programming paradigm.
questions.to_find: Which algorithm design paradigm the Floyd-Warshall all-pairs shortest paths algorithm uses

understand.plain: Floyd-Warshall finds the shortest paths between every pair of vertices in a weighted graph. The question asks which design technique it uses: Greedy, Divide-and-Conquer, or Dynamic Programming. Understanding the structure of the algorithm reveals which paradigm it belongs to.

keywords.explain: An algorithm that computes shortest paths between all pairs of vertices in O(V^3) time by considering each vertex as an intermediate node.
keywords.example: For a 4-node graph, it tries all 4 vertices as intermediates to improve path estimates.

keywords.explain: A paradigm that solves problems by breaking them into overlapping subproblems, solving each once, and storing results to avoid recomputation.
keywords.example: Floyd-Warshall stores dist[i][j][k] = shortest path from i to j using only vertices 1..k as intermediates.

understand.visual_svg: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' width='480' height='160'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect x='10' y='10' width='460' height='60' class='hi'/><text x='240' y='45' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>Floyd-Warshall Recurrence</text><rect x='10' y='80' width='460' height='60' class='bg'/><text x='240' y='118' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>dist[i][j] = min(dist[i][j], dist[i][k] + dist[k][j])</text><text x='240' y='138' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>for k = 1 to n (DP over intermediate vertices)</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Floyd-Warshall recurrence showing DP over intermediate vertices: dist[i][j] = min(dist[i][j], dist[i][k]+dist[k][j]).

given.aim: Identify the algorithmic paradigm underlying Floyd-Warshall all-pairs shortest paths.

terms.meaning: Finding shortest paths between every pair of vertices in a graph
terms.example: In a 5-node graph, finding shortest path for all 25 ordered pairs

terms.meaning: Makes locally optimal choices at each step without reconsidering past decisions
terms.example: Dijkstra's algorithm greedily picks the nearest unvisited vertex
terms.connects: Floyd-Warshall does NOT use this; it reuses stored subproblem results

terms.example: Adding vertex k as allowed intermediate and checking if it improves path i->j
terms.connects: This is the classic DP formulation of Floyd-Warshall

given.plan: Analyze Floyd-Warshall's structure: it uses a 3D DP state where subproblems are shortest paths using a subset of vertices as intermediates, solving subproblems in order and reusing them.

steps.title: Analyze Floyd-Warshall structure
steps.apply: Floyd-Warshall defines subproblems: let dist[i][j][k] be the shortest path from i to j using only vertices {1, 2, ..., k} as intermediates. Base case: dist[i][j][0] = direct edge weight (or infinity if no edge). For each k from 1 to n, update using the recurrence. The algorithm fills a 3D table in order of increasing k.
steps.note: This is the hallmark of dynamic programming: overlapping subproblems solved in bottom-up order.

steps.title: Eliminate other paradigms
steps.apply: Greedy: Floyd-Warshall revisits and updates all pairs for each intermediate vertex - not locally greedy. Divide-and-conquer: the subproblems overlap (dist[i][k] and dist[k][j] are reused), which distinguishes DP from D&C. Floyd-Warshall clearly matches Dynamic Programming: it has optimal substructure and overlapping subproblems, solves them in bottom-up order, and stores intermediate results.
steps.note: Dijkstra is greedy (single-source); Floyd-Warshall is DP (all-pairs).

questions.formula_note: Floyd-Warshall uses DP recurrence over intermediate vertices: dist[i][j][k] = min(dist[i][j][k-1], dist[i][k][k-1] + dist[k][j][k-1]). This exhibits both optimal substructure and overlapping subproblems.

questions.question:
Which of the following statements is false?
A. Optimal binary search tree construction can be performed efficiently using dynamic programming.
B. Breadth-first search cannot be used to find connected components of a graph.
C. Given the prefix and postfix walks over a binary tree, the binary tree cannot be uniquely constructed.
D. Depth-first search can be used to find connected components of a graph.
questions.answer: B. Breadth-first search cannot be used to find connected components of a graph.
questions.to_find: Which statement among A, B, C, D is FALSE

understand.plain: We need to evaluate four statements about classic algorithms (optimal BST, BFS/DFS on graphs, and tree reconstruction from traversals) and identify which one is incorrect. Three statements are true and one is false.

keywords.explain: Maximal subgraphs where every two vertices are connected by some path.
keywords.example: A graph with nodes {1,2,3} where 1-2 are connected but 3 is isolated has 2 connected components.

keywords.explain: Explores graph level by level; visits all neighbors of current node before moving deeper.
keywords.example: BFS from node A visits all nodes reachable from A; repeating for unvisited nodes finds all components.

keywords.explain: Prefix (preorder) visits root before children; postfix (postorder) visits root after children.
keywords.example: Tree with root A, left B, right C: preorder=ABC, postorder=BCA. These two together cannot uniquely determine the tree.

understand.visual_svg: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' width='500' height='200'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect x='10' y='10' width='480' height='35' class='ok'/><text x='20' y='33' class='tag ink' style='fill:white'>A. TRUE: Optimal BST uses DP (Knuth's algorithm)</text><rect x='10' y='50' width='480' height='35' class='no'/><text x='20' y='73' class='tag ink' style='fill:white'>B. FALSE: BFS CAN find connected components</text><rect x='10' y='90' width='480' height='35' class='ok'/><text x='20' y='113' class='tag ink' style='fill:white'>C. TRUE: Preorder+Postorder cannot uniquely determine tree</text><rect x='10' y='130' width='480' height='35' class='ok'/><text x='20' y='153' class='tag ink' style='fill:white'>D. TRUE: DFS can find connected components</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Four statements labeled A to D with TRUE/FALSE assessment. Statement B is the false one.

given.aim: Identify the false statement among four claims about graph algorithms and tree algorithms.

terms.meaning: Knuth's DP algorithm constructs an optimal BST in O(n^2) or O(n^3) time
terms.example: For keys with given search probabilities, DP finds the BST minimizing expected search cost

terms.meaning: BFS from an unvisited node marks all reachable nodes; iterating over all nodes finds all components
terms.example: Standard BFS-based component finding: for each unvisited node, do BFS and label a new component
terms.connects: Statement B is FALSE (BFS CAN find connected components)

terms.meaning: Knowing only preorder and postorder traversals is insufficient to uniquely reconstruct a binary tree
terms.example: A node with only a left child vs only a right child can produce the same preorder and postorder

terms.meaning: DFS from each unvisited node labels all reachable nodes as one component
terms.example: Standard DFS-based component finding is taught in every algorithms course

given.plan: Evaluate each statement: A (DP for optimal BST = true), B (BFS cannot find components = FALSE, it CAN), C (preorder+postorder not unique = true), D (DFS finds components = true). The false statement is B.

steps.title: Evaluate statements A, C, D
steps.apply:
Statement A: Optimal BST uses DP (Knuth-Yao algorithm). TRUE.
Statement C: Given only preorder and postorder of a binary tree, the tree may not be uniquely determined. For example, if a node has only one child, we cannot tell if it is left or right child from preorder+postorder alone. TRUE.
Statement D: DFS can find connected components by running DFS from each unvisited node and grouping. TRUE.
steps.note: Statements A, C, D are all true.

steps.title: Evaluate statement B
steps.apply: Statement B claims: 'BFS cannot be used to find connected components of a graph.' This is FALSE. BFS is perfectly capable of finding connected components: start BFS from any unvisited vertex, mark all reachable vertices as one component. Repeat for remaining unvisited vertices. This is the standard BFS-based connected components algorithm, running in O(V + E) time. Both BFS and DFS can find connected components of an undirected graph.
steps.note: The false statement is B. BFS absolutely CAN find connected components.

solution.result: B. Breadth-first search cannot be used to find connected components of a graph. (This statement is FALSE.)

questions.formula_note: BFS and DFS both find connected components in O(V+E). Optimal BST uses Knuth's DP. Preorder+postorder alone cannot uniquely reconstruct a binary tree (inorder is needed with either).
